ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for white bread with cheese and kaya; mapped to broader bread food product class as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for white bread with cheese and kaya; A000N (Bread and similar products) used as the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Singaporean bread dish.

serving.description: 2 slices of white bread with 1 slice of cheese and kaya spread
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of two slices of white bread (approximately 60–70 g) spread with kaya and topped with one slice of processed cheese, totalling approximately 100 g.

nutrient_source.title: White bread with cheese and kaya — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for white bread with cheese and kaya (cr_id F05020122). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite analogy (white bread + processed cheese + kaya components) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for white bread with cheese and kaya as a composite dish. GI of 71 is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for white wheat bread (GI ~71, glucose reference). Cheese and kaya toppings do not substantially alter the GI of the bread base; kaya adds sugar which may marginally elevate postprandial response but the bread dominates the carbohydrate load. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (71) × available carbohydrate per serving (32.88 g per 100 g × 100 g serving) / 100 = 23.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content at 692 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 600 mg threshold, combined with negligible potassium (13 mg per 100 g), creating an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this combination; if consumed, limit portion to one slice and choose low-sodium cheese alternatives, as the 692 mg sodium per 100 g significantly exceeds safe limits for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) refined white bread base combined with 32.88 g carbohydrate per 100 g, 7.37 g added sugar from kaya, and only 1.45 g dietary fibre, producing significant postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this food; if consumed, restrict to a very small portion, pair with a protein source to blunt glycaemic response, and consider substituting wholegrain bread with a minimal kaya spread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (692 mg per 100 g) from processed cheese, saturated fat from both cheese and kaya coconut milk, and added sugar from kaya collectively elevate cardiovascular risk through blood pressure, lipid, and inflammatory pathways.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this combination due to its high sodium, saturated fat, and added sugar content; if desired, substitute with wholegrain bread, a small amount of avocado or nut butter, and omit processed cheese.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (692 mg per 100 g) worsens tissue oedema, kaya contains coconut milk high in pro-inflammatory saturated fat, and the refined carbohydrate base promotes systemic inflammatory mediators.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this combination; the high sodium, saturated fat from coconut in kaya, and refined carbohydrate base collectively promote inflammation and may worsen joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White bread with cheese and kaya is a popular Singaporean breakfast combination consisting of sliced white sandwich bread spread with kaya — a sweet, fragrant coconut and egg jam — and topped with one or more slices of processed cheese. Kaya (also spelled 'kaya' from the Malay word meaning 'rich') is a traditional Southeast Asian jam made from coconut milk, eggs, and sugar, flavoured with pandan leaf, and is a staple of the Singaporean kopitiam (traditional coffee shop) breakfast culture. The combination of sweet kaya and savoury cheese on soft white bread creates a distinctive sweet-savoury flavour profile with a soft, pillowy texture. Nutritionally, this dish provides 242 kcal per 100 g, with a notably high protein content of 14.2 g per 100 g largely attributable to the processed cheese component, and 32.88 g carbohydrate per 100 g from the white bread and kaya sugar. The sodium content is very high at 692 mg per 100 g, primarily from the processed cheese, making this a high-sodium food. The glycemic index is high (approximately 71), driven by the refined white bread base and the added sugars in kaya, with a glycemic load of approximately 23 per 100 g serving. Dietary fibre is low at 1.45 g per 100 g. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, high GI, low fibre, and saturated fat content. It is commonly consumed at breakfast or as a morning snack in Singapore and Malaysia, often accompanied by kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea).

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020122) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. The food is a canonical Singaporean breakfast item with well-documented nutritional composition. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component analogy and documented accordingly. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database white bread reference value, which is the closest defensible approved-source analogue.
notes: G3 SENTINEL DOCUMENTATION — The following per_100g fields are set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), full micronutrient panel (vitamins B1–B12, folate, vitamins C/D/E/A), and moisture data for this composite Singaporean bread dish are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F05020122. No sufficiently close composite match providing these values was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or other approved sources as of 2026-04-16; therefore -1 is used to indicate data not available rather than a confirmed zero. | SATURATED FAT ESTIMATION: saturated_fat_g of 2.8 g per 100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component analogy: white bread (~0.6 g sat fat per 100 g) + processed American cheese (~14 g sat fat per 100 g, weighted ~15% of dish) + kaya (~2 g sat fat per 100 g from coconut milk, weighted ~20% of dish), yielding approximately 2.8 g per 100 g; this is an approximation and should be verified against direct laboratory analysis. | POTASSIUM NOTE: potassium value of 13 mg per 100 g is notably low and is preserved as reported by HPB; this reflects the dominance of white bread and kaya in the composition with limited potassium-rich ingredients. | CALCIUM NOTE: calcium value of 750 mg per 100 g is high and consistent with the processed cheese component contributing significant calcium. | CORE MACROS: all core macros (protein_g 14.2, fat_g 6.17, carbohydrate_g 32.88) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
